skill.name: web-accessibility
skill.description: Web accessibility compliance specialist. Use when conducting WCAG compliance audits, testing screen reader compatibility, validating keyboard navigation, or ensuring inclusive design. Focuses on WCAG 2.1/2.2 standards.
skill.summary: Audit websites and interfaces for WCAG accessibility issues and practical remediation steps.
skill.author: Joseph OBrien

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported external command and weak cryptography patterns, but the evidence is Markdown documentation in SKILL.md. The backtick alerts are fenced prompt and markup examples, and the weak cryptography alerts are accessibility prose with no cryptographic API. No evidence found for prompt injection, data exfiltration, malware behavior, network use, filesystem access, or executable scripts.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Fences Flagged as External Commands
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE. The flagged locations are Markdown code fences, prompts, and HTML examples inside documentation. SKILL.md contains no Ruby backtick execution, shell invocation, subprocess API, or instruction to execute host commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding context shows fenced Markdown examples only. There is no executable Ruby, shell code, or command runner in the single scanned file.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Accessibility Text Flagged as Weak Cryptography
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE. The flagged lines are accessibility descriptions and WCAG guidance, not cryptographic operations. No encryption, hashing, cipher selection, or security-sensitive credential handling appears in SKILL.md.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found prose about WCAG, inclusive design, descriptions, and remediation. These strings explain accessibility practices and do not implement or recommend weak cryptography.

content.value_statement: Accessibility issues can block users and create compliance risk. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code review WCAG issues and produce practical fixes.
actual_capabilities: Guides WCAG 2.1 and 2.2 audits across A, AA, and AAA criteria.
Reviews semantic HTML, ARIA usage, headings, labels, and form errors.
Checks keyboard navigation, focus handling, and screen reader compatibility.
Assesses color contrast and recommends accessible color changes.
Evaluates alternative text, captions, media descriptions, and inclusive design gaps.
Produces issue-focused remediation notes with severity, locations, and fixes.
limitations: Does not run automated browser tests by itself.
Requires page source, screenshots, URLs, or user-provided findings for analysis.
Does not certify legal compliance or replace expert human testing.
Screen reader behavior may need validation in real assistive technologies.

use_cases.title: Review a Feature Before Release
use_cases.description: Find WCAG issues in a new page or component before it reaches production.

use_cases.title: Improve Inclusive Interface Design
use_cases.description: Check color, labels, structure, and interaction states against accessibility expectations.

use_cases.title: Prepare an Accessibility Remediation Plan
use_cases.description: Convert audit notes into prioritized fixes for planning, tracking, and stakeholder review.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Accessibility Review
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner review of one page or screen.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this page for common accessibility issues. Focus on headings, image text alternatives, form labels, keyboard access, and color contrast.

prompt_templates.title: Keyboard and Focus Check
prompt_templates.prompt: Test this interface for keyboard navigation and focus management issues. Identify missing focus states, tab order problems, traps, and unreachable controls.

prompt_templates.title: WCAG AA Audit
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit this feature against WCAG 2.2 Level AA. Group findings by severity, cite the relevant guideline, and suggest specific remediation steps.

prompt_templates.title: Release Remediation Plan
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced planning for a product team.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a prioritized accessibility remediation plan for these findings. Separate release blockers, high-impact fixes, validation steps, and follow-up manual tests.

output_examples.input: Audit a homepage for WCAG compliance.
output_examples.output: The review lists missing image alternatives, unlabeled form fields, weak button contrast, and heading order problems. Each issue includes severity, affected area, WCAG level, and a recommended fix.

output_examples.input: Check a checkout flow for keyboard accessibility.
output_examples.output: The response identifies controls skipped by tab order, unclear focus indicators, a modal focus trap, and validation messages not announced to assistive technology.

output_examples.input: Turn audit notes into a remediation plan.
output_examples.output: The plan separates immediate blockers from lower-risk improvements, assigns validation methods, and recommends manual checks with keyboard-only and screen reader testing.

best_practices: Provide source, screenshots, or clear interaction descriptions for the page under review.
Ask for WCAG level and product context before requesting a final issue list.
Validate important findings with keyboard-only testing and assistive technology.
anti_patterns: Do not treat automated checks as complete accessibility certification.
Do not add ARIA when semantic HTML already provides the correct behavior.
Do not defer keyboard access, labels, or contrast fixes until after release.

faq.question: What WCAG versions does this skill focus on?
faq.answer: It focuses on WCAG 2.1 and 2.2 across A, AA, and AAA levels.

faq.question: Can it test screen readers directly?
faq.answer: It can guide screen reader checks and review semantic patterns, but real assistive technology testing is still needed.

faq.question: Can it review designs before code exists?
faq.answer: Yes. It can review screenshots, design descriptions, color choices, labels, flows, and interaction requirements.

faq.question: Does it replace automated accessibility scanners?
faq.answer: No. It complements scanners by explaining issues, prioritizing fixes, and identifying concerns that tools may miss.

faq.question: What inputs work best?
faq.answer: Useful inputs include page source, component code, screenshots, URLs, audit notes, keyboard behavior, and known user flows.

faq.question: Can it provide legal compliance approval?
faq.answer: No. It supports accessibility review, but legal compliance decisions require qualified professional review.
